post_mortem: The Tigers won 2-1, so the thesis was directionally correct — Detroit got the W. But -1.5 requires a 2-run margin, and the game stayed a 1-run contest wire to wire. Per the play-by-play, LAA scored in the 1st inning and held that 1-0 lead through 8 innings of near-total offensive silence from Detroit (0 runs across innings 1-8). The Tigers' only offense came on a Hao-Yu Lee two-out, two-run double in the top of the 9th off Kirby Yates — a walk-off rally that flipped a 1-0 deficit into a 2-1 win, but left zero margin for the run line. The Angels' bullpen — which ranked as the best in baseball over the prior 14 days (2.61 SIERA, 31.2% K%) — held Detroit scoreless until the final out of the 9th, exactly the scenario the pick's 'case against' flagged but underweighted.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The pick won the game, lost the run line by exactly 1 run — pure margin variance, not a systematic model failure.

post_mortem: The entire thesis was built on Sugano (4.80 ERA, 1.71 HR/9) and Lowder (4.91 ERA) as the two mediocre arms. Neither started. Brady Singer went seven strong innings allowing four hits, and Gabriel Hughes held Cincinnati to two runs through 5.1 innings — two competent performances the model never priced in. The final was 7-2 (9 total), a full 3.5 runs short of the 12.5 over. Coors Field never got a chance to do its job because the starters the pick was predicated on were not on the mound.
candidate_rule: Before posting any total pick, verify the confirmed starter via MLB.com or the team's official transaction wire within 2 hours of first pitch — if the named starter has changed, void or re-evaluate the pick from scratch; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but starter-swap invalidations are a known, recurring failure mode worth tracking as a pattern.

post_mortem: Witt went 0-for in Game 2 (July 18, 4:10 PM, Canning starting) with 0 total bases, exactly the 0-for-4 scenario the 'case against' acknowledged but dismissed. The thesis leaned hard on Canning's 6.47 ERA and weather boost while ignoring a flagged structural problem: Canning held the platoon advantage over Witt, as both are right-handed — a factor pre-game analysis explicitly noted. A right-handed bat facing a righty pitcher, even a bad one, is a materially worse matchup than the ERA-only framing suggested, and Canning's sinker-heavy approach (38.3% usage) generates ground balls, not the hard-contact extra-base hits the model needed. The thesis picked the right pitcher to attack but the wrong hitter to do it with.
candidate_rule: When targeting a player prop against a weak starter, verify platoon split alignment before entry — if the hitter faces a same-handed pitcher, require a minimum platoon-adjusted wOBA threshold (e.g., .370+) before approving the pick at juice above -120. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor for pattern.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One 0-for game against a bad pitcher is noise, but the platoon oversight is a repeatable model flaw worth tracking.

post_mortem: The thesis was executing perfectly: Cole was dominant through six innings, holding the Dodgers hitless and the Yankees led 1-0 on an unearned run off Sasaki in the fourth. The loss came down to a single pitch — Cole at 90 pitches to start the seventh, walked Mookie Betts, Boone visited the mound and left Cole in, and Muncy hammered a hanging 2-2 slider into the right-field second deck for a go-ahead two-run shot (his 18th HR). The Yankees' best scoring chance in the eighth died when Grisham was gunned down at the plate on a Ben Rice double via an Andy Pages-to-Betts relay. Tanner Scott closed it out in the ninth. The pick was right — Cole outpitched Sasaki, the Yankees generated traffic — but one left-over slider to a hot hitter ended it.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean VARIANCE loss on a single hung slider in the seventh; no systemic flaw to correct.

post_mortem: The thesis correctly identified Nola as HR-prone — the Mets hit three solo shots off him — but the model over-weighted run volume and under-weighted the scenario where all damage is solo and the Phillies offense goes completely cold (4 hits, 1 run). Christian Scott walked nobody and struck out seven, the exact opposite of his 11.2% BB% projection. The over's only lifeline was a bases-loaded, no-out jam in the 5th that Nola escaped via a line-drive double play, and Schwarber's would-be tying HR that hit the right-field railing and stayed in play. The pick was built on run accumulation from both sides; only one side produced, and only in solo-HR drips.
candidate_rule: When the over thesis depends on a high-BB% starter walking batters into trouble, require a minimum sample of 3+ recent starts showing elevated BB rates before treating it as a reliable game-day input — one-start or season-long BB% can mask recent command improvements (N=1, insufficient to establish rule).
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the solo-HR trap (runs scored without run accumulation) is a recurring over-killer worth tracking across future Nola starts.

post_mortem: Nola leaned on his curveball and changeup all night — 14 of 16 whiffs came on secondaries — neutralizing the four-seam fastball thesis, but Soto still managed contact. The pick died when Soto was pulled in the 8th inning with left calf soreness, ending his night at 1 total base before he could accumulate. The Mets won 4-1 and the game environment was fine; Soto's mid-game injury exit was the decisive, unforeseeable event.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Soto's calf injury is an isolated event; monitor his health status before future prop exposure.

post_mortem: The under was very much alive through seven innings — the game sat at TOR 3, SD 3 after Gausman's solid 6-inning, 3-ER outing, and Toronto scratched ahead 4-3 in the top of the 8th on a Jonatan Clase RBI off Adrian Morejon. The under died in the bottom of the 8th against Toronto closer Jeff Hoffman: Manny Machado tied it at 4-4, then Ty France walked it off at 5-4 to push the total to 9. The pick was correctly structured — Gausman delivered, Márquez was pulled early and managed, and the game was under the number heading into the final two innings.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean VARIANCE loss driven by a single two-run 8th inning off the closer; no systemic flaw to correct.

post_mortem: The under was killed by a freak injury to the pick's own anchor: Emerson Hancock took a hard comebacker off his bare right hand on his very first pitch of the game and was forced to leave in the second inning with a right-hand contusion, turning it into an unplanned bullpen day for Seattle. With Hancock gone early, the Mariners' pen leaked runs freely — Weston Wilson homered to lead off the fourth to end Seymour's outing, then Randy Arozarena followed with a three-run opposite-field homer to make it 7-0, and Seattle cruised to an 8-2 final. The thesis on Seymour was directionally correct (he was knocked around and exited early), but the catastrophic run-scoring was driven entirely by Seattle's bullpen exposure after Hancock's injury — an unforeseeable in-game event that no pre-game model could have priced.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Starter injury on pitch #1 is a black-swan event, not a systematic modeling gap.

post_mortem: Bradley went 7 IP and struck out exactly 6 — one short of the 6.5 line. The Angels made contact all game rather than chasing, and a key 6th-inning double play off Jo Adell (a ground ball, not a strikeout) killed what could have been a multi-K at-bat. Bradley was pulled after Guzman's solo homer in the 7th ended his night at 6 Ks with no further opportunity to push over the line. The thesis was sound — the Angels just didn't swing and miss at the rate the K% implied on this specific day.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — Bradley's season K rate and the matchup logic remain valid; this was a one-start contact variance outcome.

post_mortem: Boyd generated only 8 swinging strikes across his entire outing — the Reds made contact relentlessly, posting a 10-6 GB/FB ratio against him and forcing weak grounders rather than swings-and-misses. The thesis leaned on Boyd's season-long 26.2% K rate but ignored his 5.19 road ERA, which signaled his stuff plays down away from Wrigley. With only 8 whiffs on the day, reaching 6 Ks was never realistic regardless of innings pitched.
candidate_rule: Before rostering a pitcher K prop on the road, cross-check road K rate or road ERA split — if road ERA is 1.5+ runs higher than home ERA, flag as a degraded-stuff environment and require a larger edge to justify the juice; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one start where Boyd's road split bit us does not confirm a systemic pattern, but the 5.19 road ERA was available pre-game and was not weighted in the thesis.

post_mortem: The thesis was built entirely on Yamamoto dominating and Pfaadt imploding — neither happened. Per the ESPN box score, Yamamoto faced 21 batters and posted a Game Score of 63, meaning he was merely average, not unhittable; Arizona scored 9 runs against him and the Dodger bullpen. Pfaadt, the supposed liability, posted a Game Score of 65 and held the Dodgers to 0-for-3 with RISP, with the team stranding only 2 baserunners all game. The pick was a classic 'fade the bad pitcher, ride the ace' model that ignored a critical hidden data point: Pfaadt had just thrown five scoreless innings in relief against San Diego on Tuesday and was quietly trending up, while the thesis recycled season-long ERA numbers without accounting for his recent form (4.03 ERA over his last 22.1 IP, not the disaster the headline implied). The model also ignored the pre-game context that the Dodgers' bullpen was beleaguered after burning seven arms in Game 1 the night before — a factor that amplified any Yamamoto early exit.
candidate_rule: Before fading a starter on ERA alone, cross-check their last 3 starts for trend direction — a pitcher with a bad season ERA but two consecutive quality starts is not the same bet as one in active freefall; apply this filter before publishing the thesis (N=1, insufficient to establish rule).
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one game where the 'bad' starter outpitched the ace is noise, not signal.

post_mortem: Ryan went exactly 6 innings, allowed 2 runs on 6 hits, and finished with 6 strikeouts — one short of the 7.5 line. The game stayed competitive (Twins won 5-3), so he had the runway, but the Angels made contact all day: Ryan posted a 3 GB / 9 FB split, indicating a put-it-in-play approach rather than a swing-and-miss one. The core model error was ignoring the margin: Ryan's 10.5 K/9 rate over 6 innings projects to ~6.1 Ks — essentially a coin flip at 7.5, not the 'free money' the thesis claimed.
candidate_rule: When a pitcher's projected K total (K/9 × expected IP) lands within 1.0 of the line, flag as thin-edge and require a K% vs. opponent strikeout rate crosscheck before recommending the over — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One razor-thin miss does not confirm a systematic projection error, but the margin math should be reviewed on future K props.

post_mortem: The thesis leaned on Boston's season-long R/G (4.09, 24th in MLB) but ignored a critical split: the Red Sox were scoring 4.60 R/G on the road — well above the number used to justify the under. McLean's known structural flaw (3.29 BB/9, 37 walks in 101.1 IP) against a patient Boston lineup materialized: Seigler and Abreu each hit two-run homers, Yoshida doubled twice and drove in two, and Boston put up 6 runs on McLean and the bullpen. Gray was dominant (1 R, 5 K in 6 IP), so the Mets' half of the total was fine at 2 — the loss came entirely from underestimating Boston's road offense and discounting McLean's walk/traffic problem against a disciplined lineup.
candidate_rule: When projecting team offense for totals, use road/home splits rather than season-average R/G — if a team's road R/G exceeds their season average by 0.4+ runs, flag the discrepancy before applying the season number to an away game; validate against N≥10 road games in the current season.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one loss on a road-split oversight is noise, not pattern.

post_mortem: The entire thesis was built on Hunter Greene being unavailable or ineffective — but Greene was never actually scratched. He went out and dominated, striking out 12 Cubs in 7 scoreless innings (game score: 82), making the 'bullpen game incoming' premise completely wrong. Meanwhile, Imanaga went only 5 innings, fanning exactly 5 batters — one short of the 5.5 line — and was pulled after an Elly De La Cruz solo homer ended his scoreless effort in the 5th. The extended-run assumption that would have pushed him to 6+ Ks evaporated the moment Greene took the mound healthy.
candidate_rule: Before posting any prop that depends on an opponent's starter being unavailable or limited, require same-day confirmation of the pitching matchup from an official lineup source (MLB.com or beat reporter) within 2 hours of first pitch — not just a prior-start ERA signal; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the structural flaw (building a prop thesis on an opponent's starter being compromised without day-of confirmation) is worth flagging as a process gap immediately.

post_mortem: The thesis was built entirely on Bryce Miller's season-long ERA (1.71) and WHIP (0.66), but those numbers were already stale by game time — Miller entered having issued just 5 walks across his first 9 starts, yet on July 9 he walked 4 batters and surrendered 9 hits in 5 innings, giving up 6 runs (4 earned), finishing 4-3 on the night. The Marlins' offense was not 'league-average at best': Miami was batting .309 over their last 10 games and ranked first in weighted runs created, a hot-streak signal the thesis explicitly dismissed. The game was effectively over by the 4th inning — a Lopez triple keyed a four-run frame that made it 6-1, and the final 8-4 blew past the total before the 5th was complete. The model over-indexed on Miller's season ERA and under-weighted Miami's current offensive form and Miller's own career struggles against this specific opponent (6.30 ERA in 2 prior starts vs. MIA).
candidate_rule: Before anchoring an Under to a starter's season ERA, cross-check their career splits vs. the specific opponent AND their rolling walk rate over the last 3 starts — if either signals regression, discount the ERA anchor by at least one run in the total projection. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Miller clunker does not invalidate ERA-anchored under models broadly, but the opponent-specific ERA flag (6.30 vs. MIA) was available pre-game and was not weighted.

post_mortem: The entire thesis was built around Brady Singer's homer-prone tendencies and GABP's park factor — but Singer never factored into Schwarber's outcome. Jesús Luzardo started for Philadelphia, meaning Schwarber was batting, not pitching against Singer. Luzardo was dominant, throwing 7 innings of 2-hit ball with 11 strikeouts in a 1-0 pitcher's duel, and the CBS Sports play-by-play shows Schwarber's lone plate appearance of note ended in a strikeout swinging — his 1 total base came from a single at-bat, likely a walk or infield hit in a game where the Phillies managed only 3 hits total. The matchup edge (Singer's HR/9, GABP park factor) was real but irrelevant to Schwarber's total bases line, which lives and dies on his ability to get hits — and in a 1-0 game where the entire Phillies lineup was suppressed, the floor was never as high as the thesis claimed.
candidate_rule: When building a total bases prop on a power hitter, validate the opposing pitcher's identity and confirm the game environment supports offense — a 1-0 final with 3 combined hits is a structural ceiling on any total bases prop regardless of matchup data; consider filtering out props in projected low-run environments (implied team total < 3.5). N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the core flaw (park factor and opposing SP data applied to a hitter's prop without accounting for game-script suppression) is a repeatable model error worth monitoring.

post_mortem: The thesis was correct in every meaningful way — Luzardo was dominant, going 7 innings, allowing 2 hits and striking out 11, while Singer was the inferior pitcher on paper. The problem was Singer matched Luzardo pitch-for-pitch: after Bryson Stott's double in the 2nd, Singer retired the next 13 consecutive Phillies batters, and the only run of the game came on a Justin Crawford single in the 8th. PHI won 1-0 — the right team won, the right pitcher dominated — but the Phillies' All-Star lineup (Schwarber, Harper, Marsh all left in scoring position with 2 outs) couldn't push a second run across against a 5.03 ERA pitcher having his best outing of the year, leaving the -1.5 run line exactly one run short.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean VARIANCE loss where the pick was correctly reasoned and the outcome was decided by a single run against a pitcher dramatically outperforming his season ERA in one specific start.

post_mortem: The under was alive entering the seventh at 6-1 — Detmers got bombed for three HRs (Nimmo, Duran, Foscue) in a historically anomalous outing, having never allowed more than one HR in his first 18 starts. The Angels then erupted for a 5-run seventh to tie it 6-6, and Wyatt Langford — activated off the IL for a hamstring strain literally hours before first pitch — walked it off with a 9th-inning single to make it 7-6. The thesis (two solid starters, low-scoring offenses) was sound; the loss was driven by a once-in-19-starts HR barrage from Detmers and a same-day IL activation the market couldn't have priced.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Detmers' 3-HR game was a statistical outlier vs. his season profile; Langford's same-day activation was unforeseeable. Monitor for 'day-of IL activation' as a data gap in future totals picks, but one instance is noise.

post_mortem: The under died on Logan Webb's side of the ledger, not Cease's. Cease was historically dominant — 8 no-hit innings, 11 Ks, 118 pitches, touched 99 mph in the 8th — and held SF to zero runs. Webb, however, was lit up for 10 runs, surrendering a Guerrero Jr. homer, a Springer homer, and a Okamoto grand slam. The thesis leaned on Webb's season ERA (3.66) while ignoring a critical red flag that was publicly available pre-game: Webb had just allowed 7 ER on 11 H over 3 IP in his previous start at Coors Field. A starter in that kind of freefall is not a reliable anchor for an under, regardless of park factors.
candidate_rule: Before backing an under anchored by two starters, flag any starter who allowed 6+ ER in their most recent outing as a disqualifying condition — recency of form matters more than season ERA for single-game totals; validate with N≥20 such starts before hardcoding.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a directional signal only, not a validated pattern.

post_mortem: Burns walked a career-high six batters in five innings and finished with a season-low-tying two strikeouts — the Phillies were simply not swinging. The Reds won 11-5 on five home runs, including four in a single fourth inning, but Burns was burning through pitches on walks rather than strikeouts: he walked Crawford and Realmuto back-to-back in the fourth, then opened the fifth by walking Harper and Schwarber consecutively, forcing a short outing at 5 IP. The thesis was sound — Burns entered 10-1 with a 2.40 ERA and had posted 7+ Ks in 11 of his prior starts — but on this night the Phillies worked counts and took pitches instead of chasing, collapsing his K opportunity into a walk-fest that ended his night too early to accumulate strikeouts.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Burns' walk rate entering this start was 2.9 BB/9 — the six-walk implosion was a clear outlier, not a pattern. Monitor if walk rate trends up post-All-Star break before revisiting K props.

post_mortem: Webb was cooked before the first pitch was thrown. His immediately prior outing — 7 ER on 11 hits in 3 IP in a 15-3 blowout loss to Colorado on Friday — was publicly available and signaled he was not in 'elite home pitcher' form. The pick leaned on season-long ERA (3.66) while ignoring a pitcher in visible decline. Toronto made it academic in the first inning: Okamoto's grand slam off a Webb sinker put the Blue Jays up 5-0 before the Giants batted, and Cease shut SF out on 11 strikeouts over 8 innings. This wasn't bad luck — the thesis picked the wrong version of Logan Webb.
candidate_rule: Before rostering a SP as the primary thesis driver, check his last 2 starts for ER and IP; if he posted 6+ ER in either, downgrade confidence by at least 1 tier regardless of season ERA — but enforce only after N≥10 similar cases to confirm predictive value.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One blowout loss following a rough prior start is noise; pattern requires broader sample before codifying.

post_mortem: The thesis was built on a flawed deGrom data point: his season ERA entering the game was 3.48 — not the 1.28 home ERA cited in the pick — and his career line vs. the Angels was a damning 5.22 ERA in six starts, including a 9-6 blowup on May 22. The game played out exactly along those fault lines: deGrom surrendered two runs in the first on a Soler double and a Lowe single, and the under was already under pressure at 2-0 before Texas even batted. The Angels led 3-2 through six, but Sam Bachman imploded in the eighth — Duran and Foscue RBI singles followed by Osuna's three-run homer made it a five-run inning and an 8-3 final, busting the Under 7.0 by four runs.
candidate_rule: Before backing a pitcher-driven under, cross-check the starter's career splits vs. the specific opponent — if ERA vs. that team exceeds 4.50 in 4+ starts, downgrade confidence by at least one tier regardless of season-level home/away splits; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one loss does not confirm the pattern, but the data gap (season home ERA vs. career opponent ERA) is a structural blind spot worth monitoring.

post_mortem: The under was very much alive through seven innings — Miami built a 4-0 lead and Meyer/the Marlins bullpen were controlling the game. Then Seattle's eighth inning detonated: Cal Raleigh doubled in a run, Josh Naylor choppered through the right side to score Arozarena, and Naylor later scored on a wild pitch by Calvin Faucher to put Seattle up 5-4. Miami pinch-hitter Heriberto Hernández then answered with a 422-foot solo shot to tie it at 5-5, and the game finished 6-5 — one run over the total, decided entirely by a three-run Mariners eighth and a late Marlins homer. The thesis (Meyer + park + weak Seattle offense) was sound; the loss was manufactured in a single chaotic inning by a wild pitch and a pinch-hit bomb.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this was a clean VARIANCE loss driven by a three-run eighth inning and a wild pitch, not a systematic model or data failure.

post_mortem: The pitch-count ceiling thesis collapsed because Skubal's elite stuff overwhelmed the A's even within a constrained outing. He struck out the side in the first inning and finished with 9 Ks on just 96 pitches across 5 innings — getting 22 swings-and-misses on 52 swings. The A's 'contact-oriented' profile was real on paper, but Skubal's 98-99 mph heaters late in the outing exposed the flaw in the model: a limited pitch count doesn't cap strikeouts if the pitcher is generating whiffs at an elite rate per batter faced. The thesis conflated 'fewer innings = fewer Ks' with 'fewer Ks per inning,' and those are not the same thing.
candidate_rule: When a pitcher's SwStr% is elite (>15%), do not fade their K prop solely on pitch-count concerns — model expected Ks per batter faced, not just expected innings, and require the opposing lineup's contact rate to be bottom-5 (not merely bottom-10) before treating it as a meaningful suppressor; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one start where Skubal ran hot on whiff rate inside a short outing. Need to see whether pitch-count-limited starts consistently produce high K/IP before adjusting the model.

post_mortem: The under was dead before the second inning. Sánchez — a Cy Young candidate entering with a 2.00 ERA — was batted around in the first inning, surrendering six runs capped by Luke Maile's 3-run homer, the worst single inning of his MLB career. A Trea Turner error opened the floodgates further, and Sánchez was pulled after 3⅓ innings having allowed 9 ER, 12 hits, and 3 HR — all career highs in a single game — against a Royals lineup that entered with the second-worst record in baseball. The total cleared 8.5 before the second inning was over; final was 15-1.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One historically anomalous start from an elite pitcher is noise, not signal. Worth monitoring Sánchez's road splits (3 rough road starts in Milwaukee, Washington, and KC per Phillies manager Don Mattingly), but a single data point does not support a validator rule.

post_mortem: The under was dead by the bottom of the sixth. Gausman — who entered with a 6.47 ERA in June and a velocity-dipped 93.8 mph fastball — was shelled in a 5-run sixth: Devers walked, Lee reached on an error, Adames walked to load the bases, and Bericoto lined a two-run single to make it 5-1. Then, with the wind 'howling in from right field,' Heliot Ramos homered off reliever Tommy Nance to blow it open at 8-1, finishing with two homers and five RBI on the night. The park factor thesis was sound, but the pick leaned on Gausman as a 'solid veteran' while ignoring a documented red flag: he was 0-4 with a 6.47 ERA in June, his road starts were catastrophic (11 ER in 8 IP), and his fastball velocity had measurably declined. Roupp, meanwhile, cruised through eight innings on 8 pitches in the seventh after the lead was built. The under never had a chance — the game was 8-1 before the ninth — and the culprit was misrating Gausman's current form as 'solid' when the recent-start data clearly flagged him as a liability.
candidate_rule: When anchoring an under on a specific starter, weight the last-6-start ERA and velocity trend equally with the season ERA — if a starter's rolling ERA exceeds his season ERA by 2+ runs AND velocity is down, downgrade the under confidence by at least 1 tier regardless of park factors. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — single-game loss, watching for pattern of under picks relying on season-ERA averages that mask deteriorating recent form.

post_mortem: The under was alive and thriving through nine innings — Sonny Gray was dominant, taking a no-hit bid into the 8th inning (7.1 IP, 1 H, 9 K), and Rodón went 5 IP with the game sitting at a combined 4 runs through regulation. The under died in extras: Chapman blew a save in the 9th when Abreu's throwing error allowed the tying run, the Yankees then scored twice in the 10th on another Abreu error, and Boston rallied for three in the bottom of the 10th — capped by Jarren Duran's walk-off single — to push the final to 5-4 in 10 innings. Through nine innings this was a clean under; the 9th-inning blown save and a two-error extra-inning sequence by a two-time Gold Glover are the specific culprits.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a textbook extras/blown-save variance event, not a systemic modeling failure.

post_mortem: The under was firmly alive through six-plus innings — the game sat at SEA 4, CLE 1 entering the 8th, well inside the number. Cleveland's bullpen then erupted: Daniel Schneemann tied it at 4-4 off Michael Rucker, and Chase DeLauter — activated from the IL that very morning with a fractured rib — crushed a 2-run homer off Josh Simpson to make it 6-4, blowing the under in a single at-bat. The starters did their jobs; this was a late-inning bullpen collapse compounded by a roster move nobody could have priced in.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean variance loss driven by a specific 8th-inning bullpen meltdown and a same-day IL activation; no systemic flaw identified.

post_mortem: The pick modeled Flaherty as a healthy (if shaky) starter, but he was actually returning from the IL with a left peroneal strain and had made only one rehab start at Double-A Erie — that context was either missed or understated. Brown was also a pitch-count liability: his prior start lasted just 3 innings on 85 pitches, a red flag the thesis glossed over. Flaherty imploded for 5 ER in 4 IP, and Brown's early exit handed the game to bullpens that couldn't hold the line — the 12-run final (HOU 7, DET 5) was baked in the moment both starters were effectively operating as 4-inning arms coming off injury.
candidate_rule: When either starter is returning from the IL with fewer than 2 healthy starts logged, apply a minimum +0.75 run adjustment to the projected total per IL-returning arm — and flag the game for total-side caution regardless of ERA optics; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the pattern of IL-return starts inflating totals is worth tracking across the next 10 comparable matchups before codifying.

post_mortem: Nationals beat Baltimore 6-4 on June 28. Zack Littell was actually effective — he allowed only 2 runs on 3 hits over 5 innings, not the meatball outing the thesis projected. More critically, the pick ignored a glaring 2026 Henderson red flag: he ranked 329th of 343 qualified hitters in two-strike batting average (.074), was on pace for a career-high strikeout rate (31.2%), and was slashing just .214/.269/.423 on the season. The 'elite hitter vs. HR-prone pitcher' frame was built on Henderson's career reputation, not his 2026 form — and Littell's prior outing against Baltimore (5 scoreless innings in May) was available data that was not incorporated.
candidate_rule: Before targeting a player prop on an 'elite hitter,' validate current-season K% and two-strike performance — if a hitter's strikeout rate has spiked more than 5 percentage points above career norms, discount the TB Over projection by at least one tier regardless of opposing pitcher profile. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one loss does not confirm the pattern, but the underlying data mismatch (career reputation vs. 2026 regression) is worth flagging as a model blind spot to monitor.

post_mortem: The under was never really alive. The thesis correctly identified Yamamoto's dominance — he was efficient through 6 innings, facing only 24 batters and allowing just 1 run (a Gavin Sheets solo HR in the 5th). But the pick was built on a false premise about the other half of the equation: Vásquez's ERA of 4.17 masked a catastrophic recent split — a 6.91 ERA over his last five starts with a 1.94 WHIP and batters hitting .345 against him. The model used his season-long ERA as a 'serviceable' anchor when the rolling data screamed implosion risk. That implosion arrived in the 6th: Vásquez recorded only one out before being pulled, a Will Wagner fielding error opened the floodgates, and Mookie Betts capped a 9-run inning with a 3-run homer. The Dodgers added 4 more in the 8th (Tucker and Rushing each homered) to reach 15. The under was dead by the middle of the 6th inning at 10+ runs. There was also a DATA wrinkle: the Padres announced a Kyle Hart opener the day of the game, meaning Vásquez faced the Dodgers lineup cold in innings 2–5 rather than from the jump — but Hart's 2-inning stint actually held the score to 1-0, so the opener didn't cause the blowup. The core failure was treating Vásquez's season ERA as representative when his last-month ERA was nearly 7.00.
candidate_rule: When a starting pitcher's rolling 5-start ERA exceeds 6.50 and his WHIP exceeds 1.90, exclude him from under/low-total constructions regardless of season-long ERA — the regression to mean has already arrived; N=1 here, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Vásquez implosion does not confirm the pattern, but the split between his season ERA (4.17) and rolling ERA (6.91) was a red flag that was available pre-game and was not weighted in the model.

post_mortem: Lorenzen actually held up his end of the bargain — he mixed speeds and kept the Twins off solid contact all night, per Twins Daily: 'he effectively mixed speeds and kept the Minnesota bats away from solid contact.' Buxton's only plate appearance against Lorenzen in the first inning ended with him getting hit by a pitch (HBP), earning a base but zero total bases. In his later at-bat against the Rockies bullpen, he popped up a hanging sweeper to end a rally. Colorado won 8-5 behind Hunter Goodman's 3-homer, 5-RBI night — the Twins lost the game, but the prop died specifically because Lorenzen, despite his 7.11 ERA, was sharp on this particular night and Buxton's two plate appearances produced an HBP and a popup. The matchup thesis was sound; Lorenzen simply had one of his better outings on the wrong day.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. A pitcher with a 7.11 ERA will occasionally have a good start — this is the definition of variance, not a model flaw.

post_mortem: The wind-suppression thesis was built around preventing home runs, but the game blew up on contact hits, not power. Teng loaded the bases in the 3rd via a walk, an Altuve error, and an HBP, then hung a curveball that Kerry Carpenter crushed for a grand slam to put Detroit up 4-3. Valdez's own line was a disaster — 6 IP, 8 H, 4 ER — as the Astros peppered him with singles and doubles rather than homers, and the Detroit bullpen then surrendered the lead late, finishing 8-6. The model correctly identified HR suppression but failed to account for the fact that both pitchers' elevated walk rates (flagged in the 'case against') would create traffic that contact-heavy offenses could exploit without needing the long ball at all.
candidate_rule: When both starters carry BB rates above 8.5%, discount wind-based HR suppression signals by at least 50% on totals, because base-on-balls traffic enables run-scoring without home runs — but N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The walk-rate vulnerability was explicitly noted in the 'case against' and was the actual mechanism of failure; watching for pattern before codifying.

post_mortem: The under was dead by the end of the 2nd inning. Skenes — who entered 5-0 with a 0.53 ERA lifetime vs. Cincinnati — imploded in the second, surrendering four runs on RBI singles to Steer and Stephenson plus sacrifice flies from Dunn and De La Cruz, burning 38 pitches in that one frame alone and forcing an early exit after just five innings. The game was tied 4-4 when he left, but pinch-hitter Noelvi Marte broke it open with a 405-foot two-run homer off Mason Montgomery in the 8th, pushing the final to 6-4. The thesis leaned entirely on Skenes being Skenes, but ignored a critical red flag hiding in plain sight: he had posted a 4.03 ERA over his prior seven starts (0-5 record) and had already shown a pattern of second-inning blow-ups in his three 'bad' starts this season. The model treated his season ERA (2.86) as representative of his current form when his recent-start ERA told a very different story.
candidate_rule: When a starter's rolling 7-start ERA (4.03) diverges from his season ERA (2.86) by more than 1.00 run, discount the season-long number and re-price the total using the recent-form ERA — N=1 loss on this specific pattern, insufficient to establish a rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One Skenes blow-up game does not confirm a regression pattern; watching for recurrence over next 5 starts before formalizing.

post_mortem: The thesis leaned on Petco Park suppressing offense, but the park was never the problem — the model missed a critical asymmetry in the pitching matchup. Sasaki walked five batters in four innings, surrendering a Ty France three-run homer in the 2nd after walking the first two hitters he faced, putting the Padres up 3-1 before the game was two innings old. The under was effectively dead by the 8th, when Dodgers reliever Jonathan Hernández allowed four runs on a single, two walks, another single, a double, and a final single to make it 7-1 — a total that had nothing to do with park factors and everything to do with Sasaki's well-documented road command issues (6.19 ERA on the road in 2026) being ignored in the model.
candidate_rule: When a starter has a road ERA 2+ runs higher than his home ERA AND a WHIP above 1.25, treat him as a liability for unders regardless of park factors — but validate against a larger sample before hardcoding (N=1 here).
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The Sasaki road/home split is a real and trackable signal, but one game is noise — flag for review after 3+ similar setups.

post_mortem: Schlittler was dominant — 5 IP, 0 earned runs, 9 strikeouts — and the under thesis held on the pitching side. The game was blown open by a Yankees defensive catastrophe: New York committed a season-high four errors, and all six Boston runs were unearned. The decisive blow was a Caleb Durbin two-run homer in the fifth that scored on an error-extended inning, and the Yankees' bullpen couldn't hold a 4-3 deficit in the late innings. The under died not because the model was wrong about run suppression, but because a historically bad defensive performance manufactured six runs that had no business scoring.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The loss was a pure defensive variance event — four errors in a single game is an extreme outlier, not a modelable pattern.

post_mortem: Sánchez's changeup command was off from pitch one — Washington jumped on him for four runs in the first inning, capped by a Curtis Mead solo homer, and added a fifth run in the third on a Jacob Young RBI single. Buried in a 5-0 hole with his pitch count climbing (92 pitches, 25 batters faced), Sánchez was pulled after just five innings — his shortest outing since April 7 — stranding the K prop at exactly 6, one short of the 6.5 line. The stuff was reportedly fine; the manager cited changeup command as the culprit, and two defensive errors (Realmuto throwing error, Bohm boot) burned extra pitches that further capped his inning total.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Sánchez's season-long profile (7.1 K/start, 28.5 K%) remains intact — this was a command outlier on a single pitch (changeup) combined with a brutal first inning that compressed his outing, not a structural breakdown.

post_mortem: The thesis was alive deep into the game. The Giants trailed 2-1 through five innings, then erupted for five runs in the sixth — Jung Hoo Lee's two-out, three-run triple off reliever Krook, followed immediately by Bericoto's two-run homer off Sterner — to take a 6-2 lead heading into the seventh. The Giants' bullpen then imploded across three straight innings: Langeliers singled in two in the 7th, McNeil singled in one in the 8th, and Kilian surrendered four runs with two outs in the 9th — Heim tied it, Butler put the A's ahead, and Muncy's bloop single plated two more to seal the 9-6 final. Roupp himself was not the problem; the loss was a classic multi-inning bullpen collapse after the Giants had already won the game.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a single bullpen collapse, not a systemic Giants relief failure pattern worth acting on yet.

post_mortem: The thesis leaned on Skubal's season-long ERA (3.02) without adequately discounting that he was still rounding back into form post-surgery — Bless You Boys noted he carried a 4.35 ERA and 5.42 FIP across his first two post-IL starts, with three home runs already allowed in that stretch. Tonight made it worse: Goldschmidt took him deep in the 1st inning on a 3-1 fastball, then again in the 3rd, and Domínguez hit a go-ahead two-run shot in the 6th — Skubal tied a career high with three home runs allowed, his most since 2021. The 'case against' explicitly flagged a soft pitch count and bullpen exposure, but the deeper flaw was treating the season ERA as representative of a pitcher who was clearly not yet himself. Weathers, meanwhile, held Detroit to two runs on six hits over six innings — the thesis that his HR/9 would hurt him at Comerica never materialized.
candidate_rule: When a returning-from-injury starter's last 2+ starts show a FIP 1.5+ runs above his season ERA, regress projected performance toward the recent sample, not the season line — do not back him as a heavy favorite until he posts at least 3 post-IL starts with sub-4.00 FIP. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one start does not confirm a pattern, but the pre-game data signal (4.35 ERA, 5.42 FIP in post-IL starts) was available and underweighted.

post_mortem: The thesis executed almost perfectly through six innings — Suarez was dominant (9 K, 1 walk, 13 swing-and-misses, retired the final 7 batters in order) and Boston led 6-3 heading to the seventh. The game unraveled entirely in the Boston bullpen: Marcelo Mayer fumbled a routine grounder with two outs in the seventh, keeping the inning alive, and three consecutive RBI singles off Tyron Guerrero and Danny Coulombe tied it at 6-6. Then in the eighth, with runners on first and third off Justin Slaten (0-4), Tyler Freeman dropped a walk-off bunt single to plate the go-ahead run, and Colorado added an insurance run to seal the 8-6 win. A mid-game injury to 3B Caleb Durbin (left pinkie subluxation sliding into first) also forced a defensive reshuffle that contributed to the Seigler throwing error that let Colorado back in during the third.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this was a clean bullpen/error collapse after the starting pitching thesis fully validated; no model adjustment warranted.

post_mortem: The thesis was built around two quality starters neutralizing offenses — but it was Bryan Woo who collapsed, not Ashcraft. Pittsburgh sent nine men to the plate in a five-run fourth inning against Woo, going ahead 5-1 on Endy Rodríguez's two-run double, a Tyler Callihan triple, and back-to-back RBI singles; a four-run seventh pushed it to 9-1 before garbage-time bullpen runs finished the 11-1 final. Woo had shown a pattern of extreme volatility entering this start — two scoreless seven-inning gems sandwiched around two outings where he allowed 12 runs over 11.1 combined innings — and the pick leaned on his 3.94 ERA and elite walk rate without adequately weighting his ceiling/floor spread. The under was dead by the fourth inning; this was not a late-game collapse or a freak event.
candidate_rule: Before backing an under that hinges on a specific starter's ERA, cross-check his last 4-6 starts for variance in runs allowed — if the standard deviation is high (e.g., alternating shutdowns and blowups), discount the ERA and widen the projected run range accordingly; N=1 loss, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Woo blowup does not confirm a systemic model gap, but the volatility signal was visible in the pre-game data and was not weighted.

post_mortem: Messick didn't cooperate with the contact-heavy narrative at all — he was simply dominant. Per CBS Sports, he struck out a career-high 10 batters across 7 2/3 innings, allowing just 3 hits and 2 runs on 95 pitches. The White Sox's supposed contact approach was irrelevant: Messick was working deep into the game at full efficiency, and the strikeout total blew past the 6.5 line with room to spare. The thesis that CHW's 24% K-rate vs. LHP would suppress his output was the wrong lever — it underweighted how dominant Messick's stuff is when he's locked in, and the 7.2/3 IP workload gave him far more opportunities than a typical 6-inning projection assumed.
candidate_rule: When a starter's K/9 implies ~6 Ks per 6 IP, model the over/under on innings pitched as a second variable — a pitcher going 7+ IP inflates K totals beyond the line regardless of opponent K-rate; flag any under bet where the starter has a recent history of deep outings (N=1 here, insufficient to establish rule).
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one dominant outing does not invalidate the contact-suppression thesis, but the innings-pitched multiplier effect is worth tracking across future Messick starts.

post_mortem: Lodolo was actually sharp — he held the Brewers hitless through three innings before Chourio's hard comebacker struck him on the pitching hand to begin the fourth, forcing Lodolo out after four innings with a left wrist contusion. That infield single was Chourio's only base of the night; he struck out twice on sweepers (once in the sixth chasing one way outside, once in the ninth watching a 3-2 sweeper catch the zone) and finished with exactly 1 TB. The thesis was sound — struggling lefty, hitter-friendly park — but Lodolo had his best stuff of the month, Chourio's only hit was a fluke comebacker, and the sweeper neutralized him all night.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean VARIANCE loss driven by Lodolo's uncharacteristic sharpness and Chourio's vulnerability to the sweeper on this specific night.

post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Cole being the Cole of his last five starts — but he was never sharp, surrendering 5 runs on 9 hits in just 4.1 innings. Detroit put up 3 in the 3rd (McKinstry triple, RBI singles by Torkelson and Keith), added McGonigle's RBI double in the 4th, and Greene's 422-foot homer in the 5th to make it 5-1 before the 6th inning. The under was dead by the 5th. This wasn't bad luck — the model over-indexed on Cole's season ERA (2.57) without accounting for the fact that he was pitching without Aaron Judge in the lineup to provide run support, on a Tigers team riding a 4-game win streak with real offensive momentum. Valdez, meanwhile, was the dominant pitcher the model assigned to Cole, going 6 innings of 1-run ball with 8 Ks. The pick was built on the wrong pitcher being the anchor.
candidate_rule: Before backing an Under anchored on a specific starter's ERA, cross-check his last 2 starts for hit/contact quality (xwOBAcon, hard-hit rate) — a sub-3.00 ERA can mask a pitcher who is trending toward regression; require at least 2 consecutive starts with <7 hits allowed before treating ERA as predictive. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Cole implosion does not confirm a pattern, but worth flagging that TJ-return workload management may create start-to-start variance that season ERA obscures.

post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Gavin Williams being the anchor — but he was already in a documented June meltdown before this game was even picked. Williams had surrendered 7 ER in 5 innings to Milwaukee just days earlier, and his manager said he 'wasn't himself.' On June 22, he did it again: 7 runs, 7 hits in 5 innings (both season highs), with the White Sox scoring 3 early to go up 3-0. The Guardians clawed back to tie it, but a Tim Herrin homer to Randal Grichuk broke it open late, and CHW won 6-5. The model leaned on a 3.83 ERA that was a lagging season stat — Williams had a 4.65 ERA over his last 10 starts and had given up 6 HR in his last 3 June outings. The under was dead on arrival because the pick's anchor was already broken.
candidate_rule: Flag any under pick where the primary pitcher anchor has allowed 4+ ER in 2 of their last 3 starts — season ERA is a lagging stat and recent form should override it as the primary thesis driver; validate with N≥20 before hardening.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a directional signal only, not a validated pattern.

post_mortem: The thesis was vindicated at the team level — Elder surrendered 12 hits and 8 runs in 6 innings, and the Brewers sent 11 batters to the plate in a massive 2nd inning. Chourio was right in the middle of it, but his personal contribution was an infield single to shortstop in the 2nd (his only total base) and a pop-out to first in the 4th. He scored a run and the lineup feasted, but he never squared one up for the XBH the prop needed.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean VARIANCE loss on a well-reasoned prop in a game where the underlying thesis (Elder hittable, Brewers offense active) fully played out.

post_mortem: The thesis was built entirely on Sánchez holding the line — and he did (1 ER in 6 IP, ERA dropped to 1.80). The model failed by underweighting the catastrophic downside on the Peralta side: Peralta allowed 10 hits and a career-worst 10 earned runs in just 2⅔ innings, with the Phillies scoring in each of the first three frames — Harper solo HR in the 1st, two more runs in the 2nd, then a 5-run 3rd capped by a 456-foot Schwarber bomb. The under was dead before the 4th inning. Schwarber then went 4-for-5 with 3 HRs and 6 RBI, and Harper hit for the cycle. The pick correctly identified Sánchez as elite but treated Peralta's 9.2% walk rate and 3.90 ERA as 'inefficient but manageable' — it ignored that Peralta's career-worst blowup risk against a power lineup at CBP was a live scenario, not a tail risk. A 10-run, sub-3-inning implosion is not a freak event for a pitcher with Peralta's profile in a hitter-friendly park.
candidate_rule: When the under anchor is the *away* starter (not the home ace), require the away SP's ERA at the specific road park to be under 4.50 AND his HR/9 to be under 1.20 before counting him as an under-suppressor — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Peralta blowup does not define a pattern, but the structural flaw (building an under around a pitcher with known control issues in a power park) is worth flagging for review.

post_mortem: The thesis was built entirely on Yamamoto being untouchable — but the model ignored a critical historical signal hiding in plain sight: Rogers carries a 3.48 ERA in two career starts against the Dodgers specifically, a massive gap from his season-wide 5.86 ERA. On the night, Rogers was a different pitcher entirely, allowing only one hit (Edman's two-out single in the fifth), walking two, and striking out six over seven scoreless innings while the Dodgers went 0-for-3 with runners in scoring position. Meanwhile, Yamamoto was uncharacteristically hittable — Baltimore collected six hits and drew two walks against him, scoring three runs in six innings, with the fourth-inning two-run frame aided by a deflection that inflated his final line. The pick lost not on a fluke but because the model over-indexed on season ERA and a hot streak while ignoring Rogers' Dodger-specific splits and the Orioles' demonstrated ability to make contact against elite arms.
candidate_rule: Before backing a run-line favorite on starter ERA mismatch, check the opposing starter's splits against that specific team — if career ERA vs. opponent diverges 2+ runs from season ERA, discount the mismatch thesis proportionally; N=1 against this specific Rogers/LAD pattern, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one game where Rogers outperformed his season line is noise, not a pattern.

post_mortem: Arrighetti was the problem, not Cantillo. Per the ESPN box score, Travis Bazzana hit TWO home runs off Arrighetti — a solo shot in the 1st inning and a 3-run blast in the 5th — while Kyle Manzardo added a 2-run homer in the 3rd, accounting for all 8 Cleveland runs. Cantillo, the pitcher the thesis flagged as the weak link, posted a Game Score of 71 and held Houston to 1 run. The thesis had the risk vector exactly backwards: it leaned on Arrighetti's season ERA (2.57) while ignoring that he had already yielded 3+ runs in three straight starts entering this game, a documented regression streak that was publicly available pre-game.
candidate_rule: Before backing an Under anchored to a specific starter's ERA, check rolling 3-start ERA — if a pitcher has allowed 3+ ER in each of his last 3 outings, discount the season ERA and flag as 'ERA mirage'; do not use season ERA as the primary anchor without recency adjustment. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor for pattern.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One start does not confirm ERA regression as a systematic blind spot, but the pre-game data showing three consecutive 3+ ER outings was available and unused.
